era_context: Continuation of the Late Bronze Age to Early Iron Age transition. Egypt under Ramesses XI (20th Dynasty) is in its penultimate decade. Assyria under Ashur-dan I is expanding. The Levantine coast sees the consolidation of Phoenician city-states (Tyre, Sidon, Byblos). Greece remains in the Early Iron Age (Protogeometric period). The Hittite Empire has collapsed; Anatolia is fragmented among Neo-Hittite and Luwian-speaking polities.

events.title: Continued decline of Egyptian authority in the Levant
events.description: During the reign of Ramesses XI, Egypt's grip on its former Levantine territories continues to weaken. Phoenician city-states and emerging Aramean kingdoms assert increasing independence. Egyptian military expeditions to the Levant become rare and ineffectual. This process, which began after the reign of Ramesses III (c. 1186–1155 BCE), accelerates, marking the end of the New Kingdom's imperial reach.

sources.name: Egyptian temple records and administrative papyri
Archaeological evidence from Levantine sites (e.g., Byblos, Sidon)

events.certainty_note: The general trend is well-documented; the specific year is assigned by convention within Ramesses XI's reign.

events.title: Ashur-dan I's campaigns against Aramean tribes
events.description: Ashur-dan I continues military campaigns against Aramean and other tribal groups encroaching on Assyrian territory. These campaigns are part of a broader effort to stabilize Assyrian borders and reassert control over trade routes and vassal territories disrupted by the Late Bronze Age collapse.
events.certainty_note: Ashur-dan I's campaigns are well-attested in general; specific year-by-year details are less certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Egyptian 'decline' in this period has been nuanced by recent scholarship. Some historians argue that the shift from direct territorial control to a more indirect, trade-based relationship with Levantine city-states was not necessarily a loss of influence but a strategic adaptation. The Levantine city-states remained economically tied to Egypt and maintained cultural connections, even as political independence grew.
historiographic_note: This year is poorly documented in surviving sources. The record is dominated by Egyptian and Mesopotamian elite perspectives. Archaeological evidence from the Levant provides some counterbalance but is fragmentary. The experiences of ordinary people, merchants, and displaced populations during this period of transition are largely invisible in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses XI's reign encompasses both years
